actions.thought: 8-9 offsuit is a relatively weak starting hand in a 3-handed game, even when holding the button. As this is the first hand of the tournament, I prefer to fold and conserve my 100BB stack rather than playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop or opening with a hand that has limited equity against potential callers. I will wait for a more advantageous spot to enter the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with A4o. After the Button folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Ace-high is a strong starting hand in this scenario. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and exert pressure on the Big Blind, as A4o is ahead of their average defending range.

With Pocket Jacks in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind open to 3x, I have a premium hand that is well ahead of the SB's opening range. 3-betting for value and to take initiative is the standard and best play here. I will size my 3-bet to 900 (3x the open) to build the pot in position.

Pro has 3-bet my initial open to 900. While A4o is a decent hand to open from the small blind, it performs poorly against a 3-bet range and is difficult to play out of position post-flop. Without any information on Pro's 3-betting frequency in the first hand of the tournament, I will opt for the conservative play and fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8h 2h. While it is a suited hand, it is extremely weak and bottom-of-range for a three-handed game. Opening here might work against tight blinds, but given this is a High Roller Tournament, my opponents are likely to defend correctly. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities in this 150-hand match.

With KQo in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a very strong starting hand for a blind versus blind encounter. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open to apply pressure on the big blind, gain value, or win the pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit, which has decent equity against a Small Blind open. The pot odds (200 to win 400) are favorable for a call. Since I have position post-flop, playing the hand is standard.

The flop is a very dry, paired board (Td Tc 4s). As the pre-flop aggressor, this is an excellent texture for a continuation bet, as it is difficult for my opponent's calling range to connect heavily with this flop. Betting a small size (around 1/3 pot) applies pressure and will likely fold out a significant portion of their non-paired hands, winning the pot immediately without risking too many chips. If called, I still have two strong overcards to improve.

The board is paired with two Tens, and my Ace-high has very little equity against a standard c-betting range. While the pot odds are tempting, calling with only Ace-high on a paired board out of position is a losing play in the long run. I will fold to conserve my stack for a better opportunity.
K8s is a solid starting hand on the button in a 3-handed game. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to steal the blinds or take control of the hand to play post-flop in position.

Pocket deuces are a classic set-mining hand. Given the stack depths and the button opening range, calling to see a flop is a standard play. I am out of position, which is a disadvantage, but the implied odds of hitting a set justify the call of 250 more chips to compete for a potential pot of 700+. I will exercise caution post-flop if I do not improve.

I am holding Ten-Three offsuit in the big blind. The Button raised to 300 and the Small Blind called. Although I am getting attractive pot odds of 4.5:1 to call the remaining 200, Ten-Three is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. I am likely dominated or facing better cards from both opponents, and the risk of losing more chips on later streets outweighs the potential for a small profit. I choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

I missed the flop completely with my pocket deuces on a K-high board. As the out-of-position player, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the opponent's action. Bluffing into the pre-flop raiser here is inefficient and unlikely to succeed.

I flopped top two pair (Kings and Eights) on a relatively dry board. Since Flashlight checked to me, I have a clear value bet. A half-pot continuation bet will extract value from worse Kings (like KQ, KJ, KT), middle pairs, or any straight draws, while starting to build a substantial pot.

I have pocket deuces on a King-high board with no improvement. My hand has very little equity against a button raiser who has shown aggression on the flop. Investing more chips with only a low pocket pair when there are no realistic draws or prospects for improvement is unprofitable. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better spot.

Pocket sixes is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Being on the button, I want to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or isolate one opponent. A raise to 250 is a standard size that provides value and allows me to see a flop with the betting lead in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a raise to 250. Q2o is a very weak hand to play out of position, even in a three-handed game. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against both the Button and potentially the Big Blind. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better starting hands.
Q8o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a 2.5x raise from the Button, it lacks the necessary equity, connectivity, and suitedness to defend profitably from the Big Blind. Folding is the correct play.

K8o is a strong opening hand on the Button in a 3-handed tournament. Opening for 3x the big blind allows me to seize the initiative and exert pressure on the blinds while playing in position for the rest of the hand.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, A6o in the Small Blind is too weak to call, as it plays poorly out of position and is often dominated. While it has some blocker value for a 3-bet bluff, there are better candidates in our range (like suited wheel Aces). Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ac7c, which is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard button open. The pot odds are favorable to call, and the hand has decent equity and playability post-flop, making a call the most standard and profitable play.

I am in the Big Blind and out of position against the Button. The board is coordinated (8d 9c Jh), giving me a gutshot straight draw (requiring a ten) and a backdoor flush draw. With Ace-high and reasonable equity, checking is the standard play to gauge the opponent's intention before committing further chips. If the opponent bets, I can re-evaluate my pot odds and continue if the sizing is reasonable.

I have middle pair with a King kicker on a coordinated board (8-9-J). Checking back is a solid play here to control the size of the pot and avoid a check-raise from the Big Blind, who could easily have a Jack or a straight draw. I can evaluate the turn in position.

I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw on a board of 8d 9c Jh 9h. Checking is the standard play as I lack strong showdown value and want to see a free river card. Betting is unlikely to generate fold equity against an opponent who checked the flop, and I do not have a strong enough hand to bet for value.

On the turn 9h, the board is J-9-8-9. My pair of 8s has become two pair (9s and 8s). Flashlight has checked for a second time. While I could bet for value against straight or flush draws, my hand is somewhat vulnerable to any Jack or better 9 in Flashlight's range. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and ensure I reach the river with a hand that has decent showdown value, while also avoiding a potential check-raise that would force me off my hand.

The final board is 8d 9c Jh 9h 8s, giving me two pair (8s and 9s) with a Jack kicker. This is a marginal showdown hand that functions as a bluff-catcher. Betting is unlikely to generate value as only better hands will call, and checking allows me to reach showdown and potentially win against missed draws or weaker hands.

On this river, I have made a full house (Eights full of Nines). The board is 8d-9c-Jh-9h-8s. Flashlight has checked on the flop, turn, and river, suggesting they likely do not have a Nine (which would make Nines full of Eights) or a Jack. While a Nine would beat my hand, it is statistically more likely they are holding a Jack, a smaller pocket pair, or a missed straight draw. Given the passive play from my opponent, I will bet for value, as a Jack or a stubborn Ace-high might call a moderate-sized bet.

The board is 8d-9c-Jh-9h-8s, giving me two pair (9s and 8s) with an Ace kicker. The opponent checked the flop and turn, which significantly reduces the likelihood that they are sitting on an 8 or a 9 (trips/full house). The river bet is likely an attempt to take down the pot with a marginal Jack or a complete bluff. Since my Ace kicker beats any Jack-high or smaller pair, and calling offers better pot odds than folding or trying to bluff-raise, I will call the 400 to reach showdown.

Q9 offsuit is a marginal hand, even from the button in a 3-handed game. While an open-raise to steal the blinds is a defensible play, the hand is easily dominated by better queens and typically plays poorly post-flop when called. With over 100 big blinds, there is no need to force the action with weak offsuit gappers. Folding to wait for better spots is a solid choice.

I am in the small blind with Ace-high (Ac5h) after the button folded. This is a prime opportunity to apply pressure to the big blind. Raising here allows me to potentially take down the pot immediately or play with the initiative post-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard size for this spot to force the big blind to fold or commit further chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 10s 5s. Flashlight (Small Blind) raised to 300. This is a standard Blind vs Blind situation. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, meaning I need about 33.3% equity. 10s 5s is a suited hand with some playability and should perform well enough against a wide SB opening range. I will have position post-flop, which makes calling the most attractive option to see a flop and realize my equity.

I raised pre-flop from the small blind and was called by the big blind. On a board of Jc-2h-9h, my Ace-high (Ac5h) has very little equity. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200) is a standard move to represent strength and potentially take down the pot if my opponent missed the board completely. Given that this is a relatively dry board, betting small allows me to maintain initiative without over-committing to a weak hand.

Flashlight has made a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot on a board that moderately favors his pre-flop raising range. While I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call, Ten-Five suited is near the bottom of my range and lacks sufficient equity or playability to continue. I have no direct draws and my backdoor potential is weak. Even if I were to pair the Ten or Five on the turn, I would still be in a difficult spot against many of the Small Blind's stronger holdings like Jx or better. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with QJo, which is a solid opening hand in this position. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is the standard move to take control of the pot, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially take down the pot immediately or play in position with the lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 7s, a weak offsuit connector. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300 (3x BB). While 76o has some connectivity, it plays very poorly out of position, especially against a Button opener and with the Big Blind still to act. In a 3-handed game, standard strategy suggests folding such weak offsuit hands from the SB against a raise to avoid being squeezed or playing a bloated pot post-flop with a marginal hand and no position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
Holding 4s 7c out of position facing a raise is an easy fold. This hand has very little playability and equity, making defending the big blind here a heavily losing play long-term.

I am on the button with A8o, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Standard strategy dictates opening for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard and effective sizing in this position.

I have pocket Aces, the best starting hand in Hold'em. Flash opened from the button to 300 (3bb). Being in the small blind, out of position, I need to 3-bet for value and to build the pot. A standard 3-bet sizing to 900 (3x the open) is solid and gives worse hands like broadway combos and middle/high pairs a chance to call.

Holding 3h 2s is extremely weak, and against a 3-bet in a multi-way pot, this hand has almost zero equity. Calling 800 chips to see a flop out of position with such a poor range is mathematically negative expected value. Folding is the only correct strategic decision.

I opened from the button with A8o and faced a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Pro) to 900. While A8o is a decent hand in 3-handed play, it is generally too weak to call a 3-bet out of position or even in position against a standard 3-bet range, which often consists of better Aces, pocket pairs, and high Broadway cards. This is the second time Pro has 3-bet my button open to this exact sizing (the first being Hand #1). While I suspect Pro might be 3-betting aggressively, A8o does not play well post-flop in a bloated pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity or a stronger hand to contest Pro's aggression.

2s 8d is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. From the Button, despite having position, this hand has almost zero playability and equity against any calling ranges. A fold is the standard and correct play.

With 5c6s, a weak hand in the Small Blind, and the button having folded, I am at a disadvantage post-flop against the Big Blind. Given the weak nature of the cards, folding is the most disciplined and stack-efficient decision to avoid playing a negative expected value pot out of position.
KTo is a solid hand to open from the button position, allowing me to potentially pick up the blinds or play a pot in position. A standard 3x raise is appropriate given the stack depth.

In this 3-handed game, Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300. Holding 5c 4c in the Small Blind is a marginal situation. While suited connectors have good playability, they are difficult to play out of position (OOP), especially with an aggressive player like Pro in the Big Blind who has shown a tendency to 3-bet (Hands #1 and #8). Calling here often invites a squeeze from the BB or leaves me in a bloated pot OOP with a hand that usually requires hitting the board hard to continue. Given we are early in the tournament (Hand 10 of 150), I will opt for the low-variance route and fold to preserve my stack for better spots where I have position.
J2o is an extremely weak hand and not worth defending from the Big Blind against a button raise. Out of position with a marginal holding, folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play.

I am on the button with T7s in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand in this position. A 3x raise (to 300) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and puts pressure on the blinds, allowing me to take control of the pot early and potentially win it pre-flop or play in position post-flop.
I'm in the small blind with 8c 6d, an unsuited one-gapper that plays very poorly against a pre-flop raise. Flash opened to 3x from the button, and I'm out of position. This is a clear fold.

Q4o is a weak, dominated hand, and calling a 3x raise out of position against the button is generally negative EV. While I have committed a big blind, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve stack health and avoid difficult post-flop scenarios.
K5o is a marginal hand, even 3-handed. While I could attempt to steal the blinds from the button, my hand plays very poorly post-flop if called or 3-bet. I will fold and wait for better spots.

With the button folding and myself in the small blind, I am heads-up against the big blind. Queen-Six suited is a playable hand in this scenario, but not strong enough to warrant a raise out of position. Completing the blind allows me to see a flop cheaply and evaluate the board texture against my opponent's range before committing further chips.

I have been dealt pocket eights (8h 8c), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight limped from the Small Blind after Pro folded, which often indicates a wide and potentially weak range of hands. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure, build the pot with my value hand, and take control of the betting lead going into the flop.

Q6 suited is a marginal hand and out of position. The opponent (Flash) has shown frequent aggressive pre-flop behavior by raising to 300 in multiple hands. Calling 200 to see a flop out of position is negative expected value, especially given that Q6s is often dominated by the opponent's typical raising range. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my chip stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 3c 4s, which is an extremely weak hand with no playability. There is no strategic value in playing this hand against two opponents. Folding is the standard professional play here to preserve my stack.

Flashlight has folded, so the action is on me in the Small Blind. Hand Qd 5d is a reasonable hand to attack the Big Blind with in a head-to-head situation. Raising here puts pressure on Pro and can win the pot immediately. If called, I have a suited hand with a high card that has some post-flop potential, although I will be out of position.

I hold 9s 6c in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. 9-6 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and very little equity against a typical opening range. Even though I would have position post-flop, the hand is too weak to defend profitably. The clear decision is to fold.

I am on the button with Qs4s, which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. I have been active from the button previously, and while the Small Blind (Pro) has shown a tendency to 3-bet, I should continue to open a wide range here to maintain pressure and utilize my positional advantage. If I am 3-bet, I can easily fold as Q4s is at the bottom of my opening range, but the potential to win the blinds or play a pot in position makes a raise the preferred move.
J8o is an unplayable hand from the Small Blind against a button raise. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve our stack.

Q6 offsuit is a marginal hand, but defending the Big Blind against a Button open is standard strategic practice given the pot odds of 200 to win 450. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate based on the board texture.

I have second pair with a Queen kicker on an 8-3-6 board. Since I am out of position as the Big Blind, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and evaluate my opponent's reaction to the flop. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me or cause the opponent to fold hands I currently beat, so checking is the most balanced approach.

On this dry, ragged board (8s 3h 6c), my pre-flop raising range has a significant advantage over the Big Blind's calling range. While Q-high has little showdown value, it functions well as a continuation bet candidate. Flashlight's check is standard, and a bet of around half the pot puts pressure on their range, forcing folds from better high-card hands or speculative draws that missed this flop. I will bet 300 to take control of the pot.

I have middle pair on an 8-3-6 board. While the Queen kicker is weak, it is still a pair, and Flash's bet size (300 into a 650 pot) is standard for a continuation bet. Given my pot odds of roughly 3.1:1, it is mathematically correct to call to see if the turn changes the texture or if the opponent slows down, as folding would be too exploitable against an aggressive button player.

The board has paired on the turn with an eight, making my pair of sixes even more vulnerable as a bluff-catcher. Betting here does not accomplish much, as better hands will call or raise, and worse hands will fold. By checking, I maintain control of the pot size and allow the opponent to either check back to reach a cheap showdown or bet if they are bluffing, allowing me to make a more informed decision.

The turn brings an eight, pairing the board. This card is somewhat neutral but slightly favors the caller's range as they are more likely to have middle-card combinations that include an eight. Having bet the flop with Queen-high and been called, my hand currently has very little showdown value, but the paired board reduces the likelihood that Flashlight has a trips. However, it also makes it less likely they will fold a pair of sixes or a small pocket pair to a second barrel. Checking back allows me to see a free river card, potentially improving to a pair of Queens or simply reaching a cheap showdown where my Queen-high might still beat some of Flashlight's missed draws like 54 or 75.

My hand is a pair of sixes, which is very weak on a board of 8-3-6-8-9. While I have some showdown value against total air, my hand cannot extract value from many better hands that the opponent might hold, and betting would likely cause them to fold their bluffs while calling with any pair that beats me. Checking is the most prudent path to reach a showdown cheaply or to evaluate a potential bet from the opponent, as I am out of position and have no incentive to build a larger pot with a marginal hand.

On the river board of 8s-3h-6c-8h-9c, I have Queen-high with no showdown value. After my turn check, my hand looks like a marginal made hand or a surrendered air hand. Flashlight's check on the river following a check on the turn suggests they are also likely looking for a cheap showdown with a weak pair (like a 6 or a small pocket pair) or Ace-high. While a bluff might occasionally work given Flashlight's history of folding to aggression, the board texture isn't particularly scary for their range, and my Queen-high could potentially beat some of their missed straight draws (like 54 or 75). Checking back is the most disciplined play to minimize losses in a pot where I have very little equity.

5h 6h is a suited connector that plays very well in position. Opening on the Button to 3 big blinds is a standard play to steal the blinds or take the betting lead into a post-flop situation where we will have the positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Being out of position against the button, who has opened with a raise, calling or raising with this hand would be statistically unfavorable. The best long-term strategy is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 5c, which are low suited connectors. Pro has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a total pot of 450, giving me pot odds of 2.25 to 1. Suited connectors play well post-flop, and although I will be out of position, the price is right to defend my blind against a standard button open in a short-handed game.

The flop of As-8d-Ah is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and very unfavorable for my hand (4c-5c). I have no pair and no real draw. Standard play from the Big Blind on this texture after calling a pre-flop raise is to check to the aggressor. Leading out would rarely represent a strong hand and would likely be met with a raise or a call from any Ace or pocket pair in the opponent's range.

We missed the flop completely, but the As 8d Ah board heavily favors our preflop Button raising range over the Big Blind's calling range. We can represent an Ace or simply leverage our range advantage to take down the pot. A small continuation bet of around 30% of the pot should be highly effective at folding out better high cards and marginal hands from the opponent.

The flop of As-8d-Ah is very strong for the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have 5-high with no draws and no pair. Continuing here with a call or a raise would be a waste of chips, as I have almost no equity against the opponent's betting range. Folding is the only sensible move.

Queen-four offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against two opponents. Investing chips with this holding in a three-handed game is suboptimal, as it will often force me into difficult post-flop decisions out of position if called. The most disciplined and profitable play here is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

In a Small Blind versus Big Blind scenario, J3 suited is a marginal hand that is often played as a limp (call) to see a flop cheaply, especially when the Big Blind is an aggressive player like Pro who has shown a tendency to 3-bet frequently in previous hands. Limping allows me to realize my equity without risking a large portion of my stack to a potential raise.

Q5s is a marginal hand. The small blind limped. Since I am in the big blind with a hand that plays okay postflop but isn't strong enough for a value raise, checking to see a free flop in position is the standard and most profitable play.

On a paired board with an Ace (Ad 5c 5h), the texture is very static. I have Jack-high with a backdoor flush draw. In a limped pot from the Small Blind, I am out of position against a strong opponent (Pro). Checking here is the most prudent play to see how he reacts. Betting into a dry board often gets called by any Ace or better, and I don't want to inflate the pot with a marginal holding. If he checks back, I can re-evaluate on the turn.

I flopped trip fives on an A-5-5 board. Flash checked. This is an excellent opportunity to start building the pot. Since Flash might hold an Ace and will likely call, a half-pot bet of 100 chips is a good size to extract value while keeping his range wide enough to include weaker pocket pairs or floats.

The flop of Ad 5c 5h is extremely dry and favors a range that includes Aces or Fives. Having limped from the Small Blind and checked the flop, my Jack-high holds very little equity against even a standard continuation bet or a 'stab' from the Big Blind. While the bet is small (100 into 200), continuing with J3 out of position on an Ace-high paired board is a losing play. I have no real draws other than a very weak backdoor flush, and even if a Jack falls, I could still be crushed by an Ace or a Five. Preserving my stack for better spots is the priority.

I am on the button with J6o, which is a marginal hand even in a three-handed game. While stealing the blinds is a consideration, the chip leader is in the small blind and has shown aggression in previous hands. Given the stack sizes and the weakness of my hand, I will opt to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

With JTo in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a decent hand to attack the Big Blind. Raising to 300 applies pressure to fold out weak hands and gives me the betting lead going into the flop.

I am in the big blind with 9d7s. The small blind (Pro) has raised to 300. With 100 already in the pot, I only need to call 200 to see a flop. Given the pot odds and my position, calling is the most standard defensive play against an aggressive opponent's small blind raise, as 9-7 offsuit is playable in this spot to defend the blind.

I completely missed the flop and have no direct draws. However, I have the preflop betting lead and my opponent has shown a tendency to fold to continuation bets on the flop when they miss. A half-pot continuation bet applies pressure and can win the pot outright against unpaired hands or weak floats. If I face resistance, I can easily fold.

I hold middle pair on a 4d Qh 7d board. With 300 to call and a current pot of 900, the pot odds are 3:1, which justifies a call. I also hold the 9d, giving me a backdoor flush draw. Given the opponent's aggressive betting profile as the small blind raiser, calling is the optimal strategy to keep the pot manageable while realizing my hand's equity.

The turn brings the 8 of diamonds, putting three diamonds on the board and completing many flush draws. I have absolutely nothing with Jack high, no diamond, and minimal equity. Continuing to bluff into an opponent who called the flop on this scary board is unprofitable. Checking allows me to give up on this hand and preserve my stack. If my opponent bets, I will easily fold.

I hold a pair of sevens and a flush draw with the nine of diamonds on a board showing four diamonds. The opponent, acting first, checked the turn, which may indicate a lack of strength or a trap. By checking behind, I maintain pot control and realize my equity, as I have a decent showdown hand that could improve on the river while avoiding bloating a pot against a potential trap.

I have Jack high with no showdown value on a paired, three-flush board. After giving up on the turn, attempting a bluff on the river when the top card pairs is highly risky, as my opponent could easily have a Queen or a sticky pair that they will not fold. Checking to surrender the pot and preserve my stack is the most prudent play here.

I have two pair (Queens and Sevens) with a 9 kicker. The board is paired with Queens and there are three diamonds on the board. Since the opponent checked the river, they could be checking with a weak hand, or they might be trapping with a Queen or a full house. Betting here is risky because I only get called by hands that beat me (any Queen or better) or occasionally hands I chop/beat that might fold to a bet anyway. Given the stack depths and the board texture, checking behind allows me to showdown my hand and realize my equity without the risk of being check-raised off a decent two-pair hand.
Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak hand. Being first to act pre-flop on the button in a 3-handed game, it's best to fold and wait for better opportunities to invest chips.

Pro has folded on the button, leaving me in the small blind against the big blind. Q-10 suited is a strong hand in a heads-up situation, and I have positional advantage over the big blind post-flop. Raising to 300 will allow me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or build a pot with a hand that has good equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 6h. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. While the pot odds are 2:1 and 76o is a semi-connected hand, it plays poorly out of position and is easily dominated by the Small Blind's raising range. Given that we are early in the tournament (Hand 18 of 150) and I want to avoid high-variance situations with marginal hands, I will choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous position or a stronger starting hand.

On the button, I have K4s, which is a reasonable hand to open with in late position to steal the blinds. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard aggressive move to leverage my positional advantage and try to take down the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 3x the big blind. While Ten-Seven has some marginal value in a three-handed game, playing it out of position against a raise is generally unprofitable, especially with a strong player (Pro) still to act in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit connector (4c 5h). The Button raised to 300, which is 3x the big blind. Calling here out of position with a hand that rarely flops well is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.

KTo is a strong hand on the button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and put pressure on the blinds, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes in earlier hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a 3x open raise from the Button, I have no equity or playability to justify calling or 3-betting. Folding is the only correct decision.

Holding Jack-deuce offsuit in the big blind is an extremely weak hand with very little equity. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, the hand performs poorly post-flop, especially when playing out of position against an aggressive button opener. It is better to preserve my stack for a more favorable hand rather than committing chips to a dominated range.

I am on the button with KTo, which is a very reasonable hand to open in a 3-handed game. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is my standard sizing to pressure the blinds, take control of the hand, and play in position post-flop.

I am in the small blind with King-Ten offsuit. The button has opened, and I am out of position. I am in the Small Blind with Q8o facing a 3x raise from the Button. Q8 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability out of position against a strong pre-flop raising range. Calling or 3-betting would be a losing play in the long run, so the correct decision is to simply fold.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Eight offsuit, facing a raise to 300 from the button. Given the pot odds and the frequency with which the opponent is opening, calling is a standard defensive play. I will call the 200 to see a flop and evaluate my position from there.

I have paired my eight on the flop, but with a Queen kicker, my hand is vulnerable to overcards like the Ten on the board and potential flush draws. As the big blind, I am first to act on the flop. Checking is the most standard and disciplined play here, allowing me to gauge the opponent's strength before committing more chips to the pot. I have King-Jack offsuit on a board of 10-8-3. I was the pre-flop aggressor and have two overcards to the board. I have middle pair (Eights) with a Queen kicker. The flop of 8s-Td-3s is relatively dry, but there are flush draws and straight draws (like 9-7 or J-9) that could be in my opponent's range. Given that I have a pair and the opponent is aggressive, I should call to see if the turn changes the texture or if I can reach a showdown cheaply. Folding is too tight, and raising is unnecessary as it would likely isolate me against a better hand.

The turn card 2s has put three spades on the board, making the board texture significantly more dangerous and potentially completing flush draws. My middle pair of eights, while having been decent on the flop, is now very vulnerable. Since I am out of position, betting into my opponent would likely only force out hands I beat while getting called by stronger ones. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate the opponent's action, aiming to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible.

On this turn, the 2 of spades completes a possible flush. Since I hold the Jack of Spades, I have a flush draw and also block some of Flashlight's potential flush combinations. Flashlight has checked twice now (flop and turn). While I could bet to represent the flush, checking back is a safer play that allows me to realize my equity for free. I have two overcards and a high flush draw, so I'm happy to see a free river card and potentially improve to the winning hand without risking a check-raise.

I have two pair, Queens and Eights, on a board of 8s-Td-3s-2s-Qc. The river completed the board and there is a potential flush on board with three spades. The turn was checked through, which suggests neither player had a monster hand at that point. Betting here could be risky as it might only get called by better hands or a flush that got there on the river, while worse hands that might check behind will fold. Checking is the most sound and disciplined play to reach a showdown and realize my equity safely.

On the river, the board is 8s-Td-3s-2s-Qc. I have King-high (Kh Js). After my flop continuation bet was called and the turn went check-check, Flashlight has checked for a third time on the river. My King-high has very little showdown value, as Flashlight's flop-calling range likely includes many pairs (Tens, Eights, or small pocket pairs). While a bluff could potentially represent the flush (since I checked the turn) or a Queen, it is risky because the turn check-back often looks like a marginal hand or a missed draw, which might encourage a 'hero call' from a Ten or an Eight. Given my chip lead and the tournament context, I will check back to realize whatever showdown value I have without risking more chips on a high-variance bluff.

I am holding 3d 2h on the Button, which is arguably the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite having positional advantage, investing any chips into the pot with this hand is highly unprofitable. Folding is the only sensible play here.

The button (Pro) has folded, leaving me in the small blind with 5c9c. The big blind is 100, and I have already posted 50. Completing the small blind to see a flop heads-up is a standard play with this type of suited hand, as it offers reasonable post-flop equity without committing too many chips pre-flop. I will call to complete the bet.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc6c. Pro has folded and Flashlight has completed from the Small Blind. J6 suited is a respectable hand to see a flop with in a limped pot, especially since I will have position on Flashlight for the remainder of the hand. There is no need to raise and inflate the pot with a marginal hand when I can see the flop for free.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c9c on a K-6-3 rainbow board. I have no pair, no draw, and no showdown value. Checking is the optimal strategy to minimize losses and see if the opponent checks back. Given the dry nature of the board and my lack of equity, any bet would be a bluff without much fold equity, so I will play passively.

On a board of Kd 6h 3c, I have flopped middle pair (sixes) with a Jack kicker and a backdoor club flush draw. The pot is limped (200 chips) and Flashlight, the small blind, has checked to me. My hand has significant showdown value in a small pot but is not strong enough to bet for value against a King. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and keep Flashlight's bluffs and weaker pairs in his range. This also allows me to see the turn for free and potentially improve or reach a cheap showdown.

The turn card is the 8d, making the board 3c-Kd-6h-8d. I have 9-high with 5c9c and no real equity. My opponent (Flash) checked behind on the flop, which could indicate a weak hand or a slow-played monster, but either way, my hand has no value to bet for protection or value. I will check again to keep the pot small and potentially reach a cheap showdown, or fold if Flash makes a significant bet.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a board of Kd-6h-3c-8d in a limped pot. Flashlight (SB) has checked to me for the second time. While I likely have the best hand, my hand is not strong enough to bet for three streets of value, and checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. It also avoids a potential check-raise and keeps Flashlight's weaker hands in the pot for a possible showdown or to induce a bluff on the river.

The board finished 3c-Kd-6h-8d-8c. I hold 5c-9c, which is 9-high. Both my opponent and I have checked through the flop and turn. Given that I have no showdown value and the opponent has shown no aggression, betting would be a pure bluff into a passive range. Checking is the optimal play to reach showdown and minimize any further loss, as there is a high probability the opponent has at least something (like a low pair or missed high cards) that they might check down to a showdown with.

I have two pair, eights and sixes, with a king kicker (8-8-6-6-K). In this limped pot, the action has been checked all the way to the river. Flashlight (SB) has checked for the third time. My hand has significant showdown value in this context. If I bet, it's unlikely I'll be called by any hand worse than a six (which would resulting in a chop). A king or an eight would likely have bet by now, but if they are trapping, a bet from me would only lose chips. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and win the pot against high-card hands or smaller pairs without risking more in a small pot.

My hand is 5-3 offsuit, which is extremely weak. Even in a blind-vs-blind or button situation, playing such a marginal hand out of position post-flop or getting trapped is not ideal for long-term equity. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button has folded, 97 suited is a strong enough hand to enter the pot. I have the chip lead and want to maintain pressure. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is effective for either winning the pot immediately or setting up a post-flop play with a hand that has decent connectivity and flush potential.

KTo is a very reasonable hand to defend from the big blind against a small blind raise. By calling, I keep my opponent's range wide, take advantage of the pot odds, and get to play post-flop with a positional advantage.

I have an open-ended straight draw and a flush draw on a board of 6h 5h Kd, giving me significant equity against most of Pro's range. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board contains a King, which is favorable for my perceived range, a continuation bet is the most effective play. This maintains pressure and allows me to build a pot that I have a high chance of winning if I hit one of my many outs on the turn or river.

I flopped top pair with a decent kicker on a somewhat wet and draw-heavy board (hearts, straight draws). Facing a standard c-bet from the small blind, calling is the most prudent play. It keeps the opponent's bluffs and semi-bluffs in the pot, controls the pot size given our medium-strength kicker, and allows us to utilize our positional advantage on later streets.

On the turn 9s, my hand has improved significantly. I now have second pair (nines) to go along with my heart flush draw and gutshot straight draw (needing an eight). Since I have plenty of equity and have now picked up showdown value, continuing to bet is the best play. It maintains my image as the aggressor, potentially folds out better hands like weak Kings if I continue on the river, and builds the pot for when I hit my draws. A bet of 600 (half the pot) is a standard sizing to keep the pressure on.

With top pair and a decent ten kicker, my hand is too strong to fold to a half-pot turn barrel in a blind-vs-blind dynamic. Raising would likely fold out his bluffs and isolate me against a range that crushes me (like AK, KQ, KJ, two pair, or sets). Calling controls the size of the pot, keeps his bluffs in, and lets me evaluate the river card in position.

On the river, the board pairs the King, giving me Kings and Nines with a 7 kicker. I have been the aggressor throughout the hand, but this is a dangerous card to bet into. By checking, I allow myself to get to showdown cheaply and potentially catch a bluff from Pro's missed draws (such as a heart flush or a straight draw).

The opponent showed aggression by betting both the flop and the turn. His check on the river could mean he gave up on a missed draw (like a heart flush draw) or he's trapping with a strong hand (AK, KQ, KJ, K9, or a full house). My KTo gives me trip Kings, but my kicker is only a Ten, meaning I am likely outkicked by the value portion of the range he chose to barrel on multiple streets. Since worse hands (like missed draws) will fold to a bet and better hands will call or raise, betting offers little to no value. Checking behind is the optimal play to safely realize our showdown value.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. While I have the chip lead and the button is a prime stealing position, 10-4o is a very weak hand that doesn't play well post-flop if called. With T2 suited in the small blind and the button folded, this hand is simply too weak to limp or raise. T2s has very poor playability and hot/cold equity. The most standard and profitable action here is to fold.

With A3s on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a premium hand with strong equity, nut potential, and excellent blocker value. A standard open raise to 3x the big blind (300) puts pressure on the blinds to fold and gives me the initiative going into the flop with positional advantage.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand, K-7 offsuit. Calling out of position against an opening raise from the button is strategically unfavorable, as I would be playing a mediocre hand with no initiative. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision in this spot.

I have been dealt a premium pocket pair (Pocket Tens) in the Big Blind. The button (Pro) has opened for 300, which represents a wide range in a three-handed game. I have an overpair to the middle card on a Queen-high board. Since I was the pre-flop 3-bettor, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet. I flopped top pair on a very dry A-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows Flashlight to continuation bet with their bluffs or worse hands, while keeping the pot size manageable given my marginal kicker.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (8-4 offsuit). The button has folded, meaning I am heads-up against the big blind. Playing this hand out of position is negative expected value, so the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

The river brings a fourth spade to the board. Holding the Jack of Spades, I have the third nut flush, losing only to the Ace or King of Spades. A sizing of roughly one-third of the pot should encourage lighter calls while minimizing my risk if he does happen to hold the Ace or King of Spades.

The opponent has bet 600 into a pot of 2400 on the river. My hand, pocket threes, is at the bottom of my range and is a weak bluff catcher. I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. A9o is a strong hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. My goal is to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position. I will make a standard open-raise to 3 big blinds.

I am in the small blind and out of position on a flop of 5c-8s-4c. The 5c 8s 4c flop is heavily connected and generally favors the pre-flop caller's defending range, containing many straight draws, flush draws, and made pairs. The board is paired with the turn card (5d), and I hold Queen-Jack high with no legitimate draw. Since my opponent checked the flop, they are showing signs of weakness or a medium-strength hand. Flashlight has checked to me twice on a connected board that has now paired the 5. The board shows 5c-8s-4c-5d-6h. I hold QJ offsuit. My hand has no pairs and essentially no showdown value beyond beating a complete bluff. Flashlight has checked to me on all three streets, indicating a weak hand, likely a missed draw or a very marginal pair. I am in the Small Blind with 3d 4h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. With K2o in the big blind facing a 3x raise from the button, my hand is too weak and disconnected to call or 3-bet effectively out of position. The best and most standard move is to fold.

I hold pocket Kings on a board of 8s-Qc-Js. I have a strong pocket pair, Queens, on the button. Raising is the standard play to build value and take initiative in the hand against the blinds.

95o is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the button, playing this out of position is highly unprofitable. Folding is the correct move here.
